title: RBA warns more rate rises needed in desperate inflation fight
full_text:
Details of the Reserve Bank’s desperate attempts to curb inflation and avoid a recession have been laid bare, with minutes from the board’s latest meeting revealing why members agreed to hike interest rates for the third consecutive time.
The insight comes as a senior RBA official warned the board was keeping a close eye on the fallout from the federal government’s tax shake-up.
RBA minutes, released on Tuesday, reveal that while board members agreed rate hikes could not prevent the short-term inflationary shock caused by war in the Middle East, they were needed to ease the inflationary pressures that had been building before the conflict.
“Members noted that inflation had been well above target in the months prior to the onset of the conflict in the Middle East,” the minutes say.
“The conflict had, however, resulted in a pronounced effect on the economic outlook. Members agreed that monetary policy could not prevent a near-term increase in the price level as higher fuel prices worked their way through to final prices.
“However, monetary policy could limit the risk that this cost shock resulted in a broader and sustained lift in inflationary pressure … and ensuring medium- to longer term inflation expectations remained anchored.”
The Reserve Bank raised the cash rate to 4.35 per cent at its May meeting, with eight of the nine board members agreeing to the 25 basis point hike.
On the chance of more rises this year, the minutes say the latest move would give the board “space to see how the conflict in the Middle East develops and Australian households and businesses respond”, suggesting another hike in June remains unlikely.
Economists are expecting at least one more rate rise by November but few expect a move when the RBA meets again on June 15 and 16.
“They (board members) emphasised that underlying inflation was projected to be above target for an extended period across a range of scenarios for how the conflict in the Middle East might develop,” the minutes say.
“Given this outlook, these members determined that the risks to achieving the board’s inflation objective had risen and judged that they were not confident that, at 4.1 per cent, the cash rate would be sufficient to mitigate these risks.”
Earlier on Tuesday, RBA chief economist Sarah Hunter told Bloomberg TV that the central bank would be keeping a close eye on any cooling of the property market in the wake of the Albanese government’s shake up to negative gearing and capital gains tax.
“That’s always a risk, and if we see that outcome start to play out then obviously the board will take that into consideration and be thinking about that in the context of future policy decisions,” she said.
“But as you can see in our latest forecast, we are expecting the pace of growth and dwelling construction activity to slow over the forecast horizon. And that really is consistent with those increases in the cash rate that have come through already.”
Speaking earlier at a Bloomberg forum for investment managers in Sydney, Dr Hunter said there were major risks to the central bank’s hopes that inflation would ease towards its target range by mid-2027.
“This baseline reflects several assumptions. First, the conflict in the Middle East gets resolved soon, causing some fallback in oil prices. Second, domestic capacity pressures in the economy ease. This is partly driven by the impact of higher prices on households’ budgets and spending; the increase in oil prices has made households poorer in real terms, and we expect this to weigh on household spending,” she said.
“While this is our central case, significant uncertainties remain. Oil prices could stay elevated for longer than implied by market pricing, and the Iran conflict could lead to broader, more persistent supply disruptions, adding to inflation.”
The RBA expects headline inflation to hit 4.8 per cent in the June quarter – well above its 2-3 per cent target. Its forecast that inflation will return to the midpoint of its 2-3 per cent target band by mid-2028 assumes further cash rate increases.
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
Document AUSTOL0020260519em5j000ul
summary: The Reserve Bank of Australia (RBA) has raised interest rates to 4.35% for the third time in a row to combat rising inflation, which is expected to remain above target due to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East. Economists predict at least one more rate hike by November, but not likely in June.
author: Giuseppe Tauriello
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: RBA raised cash rate to 4.35% in May.
Inflation is projected to hit 4.8% in June, above the 2-3% target.
RBA is monitoring the impact of government tax changes on the property market.
Another rate rise is expected by November, but June hike is unlikely.
ticker_line: RBA raises rates again to fight inflation
